feedback.text: The Digital Fairness Act is intended to strengthen consumer protection for digital products and services in the EU, including addressing harmful digital practices and unfair contract terms. However, a critical gap remains regarding the lifecycle and post-sale destruction of digital video games by publishers. Many publishers now terminate (or kill) gamesespecially online or service-based titlesleaving paying consumers without access to the product purchased for unlimited or even reasonable periods of use. This concerns the practice where consumers lose access to games, with no alternative provided, when support or servers are discontinued. Publishers often reserve the unilateral right to revoke or disable access to digital games, even for single-player or non-online titles, through license agreements that include terms allowing for termination at any time, for any reason. End of service for live service or online-only games typically means complete loss of accesseven for consumers who paid full price, as there is no method to continue enjoyment offline or by community hosting. There is rarely any end-of-life (EOL) provision or requirement for publishers to support continued player access or to provide an offline or self-hosted mode once official service ends. This differs starkly from the sale of physical goods, where ownership and use are expected to be retained for a reasonable lifespan. These contract terms and business practices are arguably unfair under current and proposed EU consumer law, especially as they deprive consumers of expected value and product longevity without adequate remedy. The Digital Fairness Act aims to close gaps where technological developments, online-only models, and dark patterns lead to consumer harm. The permanent loss of access to gamessometimes with short notice and no alternativesconstitutes such harm and undermines digital ownership and fairness. The issue is exacerbated by the increasing prevalence of digital-only distribution and live service models in the games industry. The Digital Fairness Act should explicitly prohibit contract terms that allow publishers to unilaterally and permanently revoke access to digital games for consumers after purchase, except where required by law or due to force majeure. Require publishers to provide an end-of-life (EOL) build or alternative reasonable access remedy to consumers when official servers or services are discontinued. Mandate clear, up-front information to consumers regarding any planned or potential discontinuation of access to service-based digital products at time of purchase. Ban non-negotiable, one-sided EULAs for games that allow destruction or loss of access to a purchased product at the publishers discretion. Ensuring the right to reasonably long-term use and access to purchased digital games is vital for consumer trust and digital fairness. The Digital Fairness Act should ensure that digital products, once sold, cannot be arbitrarily revoked or rendered unusable by the publisher without remedy to the consumer. Such provisions would align with the stated goals of strengthening digital consumer protection and addressing new forms of exploitation and harm in the digital marketplace.
